pages.name: Scene 3a · The Firm's Door — Hesren Vesh

text.content:
Scene 3a · The Firm's Door — Hesren Vesh

Hesren Vesh in his Wick Lane office. The Charter Roll of Marks is pinned behind him.

Behind the green door is the Loftwick satellite of Cindren & Vhal: a single front room, a railing, a charter of the firm's marks pinned to the wall, and one bonded clerk in dark-green livery who is courteous to the exact degree that keeps a conversation from happening.

The man who rises to meet you is perhaps thirty, neat to the point of stiffness, a crane-and-coins pin at his collar. "Hesren Vesh," he says, "for the house of Cindren and Vhal. You'll forgive me — we are a small office. I notarize, I witness, I forward. Anything of substance is Hardby's, or Rel Astra's." He says it the way a man recites the one safe sentence he's been given.

Vesh will confirm the firm's mark from the public charter on his wall and describe, blandly, what the house officially does. He cannot open any client account without written authority from Rel Astra, and he'll say so politely, twice. The scene turns on one move: showing him the marginal endorsement from Haskur's letter of credit.

The endorsement reveal

  • When the party puts the endorsement in front of him, his eyes settle on it two breaths longer than they should. Insight DC 14: he is disturbed, and hiding it.
  • Persuasion DC 18 or Intimidation DC 17: he gives up exactly four words and nothing more — "reads like Hardby work." He will not name a partner; he will not let the party take notes.
  • If they lean harder still, he excuses himself "for refreshments," and uses the minute to send a sealed message out the back by a runner. The party may notice the runner leaving (Perception DC 14) but cannot intercept without breaking civility.

GM: Vesh is not a conspirator — he's a junior clerk whose seniors are doing something he isn't cleared to know — so don't let the party break him; there's nothing inside to break. But this scene has teeth, because it does two things. It hands the party their first hard east-pointer (Hardby). And the moment they leave — or the moment that runner slips out the back — the watcher on the corner has something to report. That is one of the two triggers for the chase (Scene 3d). Tipping the firm puts a runner on the street. (Advance the Pressure Clock one segment: the firm now knows someone is asking.)

The firm at a glance

The Cindren & Vhal house seal: a crane in flight above three coins. The mark on Haskur's letter of credit and his signet ring.

  • Rel Astra (main). A four-story counting house in the Old Harbor Ward. Full vault and senior partners' offices.
  • Hardby (branch). A two-story office in the merchants' quarter; the living hand the party is hunting.
  • Loftwick (satellite). Single notary office on Wick Lane. Not a full branch; cannot hold escrow, only originate paperwork. Staffed by Hesren Vesh.

Senior partners (Rel Astra): Veshen Cindren (age 58, nephew of the founder, public-facing) and Tarlith Vhal Sereth (age 64, reclusive, manages off-balance-sheet instruments). Neither is reachable in Phase 2.

What the firm officially does

  • Bonded storage for goods in transit between Aerdi ports and inland buyers.
  • Credit lines secured against held cargo, with clearing through their own house chop.
  • Arbitration of mercantile disputes between members of allied counting houses.

What the firm unofficially does

  • Laundering of receipts and instruments — moving paper between offices until provenance is no longer traceable.
  • Identity shielding — letters of credit and passage for clients who do not wish their real names entered into Aerdi state registers.
  • Silence buying — small, regular cash payments to clerks, weighmasters, and watch sergeants near information the firm prefers undisturbed.

Caelith's working line on the firm: "Respectable enough for clean rooms. Useful enough for dirty ones."

Tactical Map

C&V Loftwick satellite — tactical map. Smaller than the Hardby branch; the strongroom is single-locked.

Scene 3b · The Dead Man's Rooms

Gilst's rooms as the party finds them — a tidy man's life left exactly as he last touched it, then ransacked.

Above the stationer's, two rooms a tidy man lived a small life in, sealed by the watch after his death — and already searched, badly, by someone else. The landlord is Doman Reith, a careful, anxious man in his sixties who has rented to clerks for thirty years and never had one die on him before.

Getting in

Getting in is easy if the party has Caelith's written request (he provides one readily, "for private inquiry, not evidence transfer"); Reith reads it twice, asks whether there will be trouble, and unlocks the door. What he says next matters:

Reith stops with his hand on the latch. "A man came up here yesterday," he says. "Had a watch tabard. I didn't know him — but you don't argue with the watch, do you. He was up here the better part of an hour. Left without a word about what he'd found. I haven't opened it since." He looks at the floor. "I should have asked for his name. I keep thinking that."

Reith isn't lying (Insight DC 13). And his "watchman" wasn't one: the tabard was Yeomanry pattern but the wrong ribbon at the cuff (Investigation DC 12), the kind a man buys from a former courier rather than is issued. Whoever searched these rooms was a professional in costume — and didn't find what the party can.

GM: The searcher was Merev Sarth, the thief who comes for the evidence later in the chapter (Scene 6), on a reconnaissance pass. She didn't have time, and didn't know exactly what she was hunting. The party can succeed where she failed. Let them feel that: the room has been gone through — drawers ajar, books shelved a hair out of true, a carpet-corner turned back and not laid flat — and Reith confirms it didn't look like this when Gilst was alive.

Layout

Two rooms: a sitting parlor that doubled as a study, and a small bedchamber. A water-closet under the stair. The parlor's east window looks down onto Wick Lane; the bedchamber's window looks onto a small courtyard with a wash-line.

There are seven things to find; each rewards a deliberate look. The prize is the second one.

A · The hollowed book

  • Investigation DC 13 on the bookshelf: the third volume of Yeomanry Constitutional Commentaries sits a finger proud of its neighbors, as if replaced in a hurry — and Gilst was not a man who left books out of true. It's hollow. Inside is a slim receipts book in his own hand: four years of small cash sums from a payer recorded only as "H.V. Hardby," forty-one entries adding to about 1,200 gp, always cash, always irregular, each small enough to pass for a clerk's bit of private bookkeeping.
  • Investigation DC 14: Three of the forty-one entries are countersigned in a different hand with a single capital initial — "E." The countersigned entries are larger than the rest (one for 200 gp; the other two for 75 gp each) and oddly timed. Gilst's notes don't say what the countersignature meant; only that on those days the payments were bigger and he wrote no reply.
  • GM: "H.V. Hardby" is the Hardby branch of the firm — Gilst likely thought he was on a junior partner's private retainer and never knew he was being paid by Cindren & Vhal proper. The "E." is the party's first brush with the paymaster above the firm. Leave it open. Caelith doesn't know the initial; Trina doesn't know it; no Yeomanry register lists a relevant figure who signs simply "E." The party leaves with a letter and a question, and that is correct. (See the @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2DMBriefJrnl.JournalEntryPage.BR2P2DMPgPlotBehind]{What Is Actually Happening} page on the DM Brief journal for the layered GM-only context that sits behind "E."; do not speak any of it at the table.)

B · The smoking gun

  • Perception DC 14 on the writing desk: a small decorative tin of throat lozenges. Real lozenges in the top half. The bottom of the tin is a false floor (Investigation DC 12 to feel the seam).
  • Under the false floor: a single signed bank instrument, made out to bearer, for 200 gp, undated, drawn on Cindren & Vhal Hardby and stamped with the very same Hardby chop as the Vandrell letter of credit. Same hand, same chop.
  • GM: This is the smoking-gun document. It puts Gilst on Cindren & Vhal Hardby's private retainer, and it links his payroll directly to the Hardby chop on Vandrell's escrow.

Caelith holds the slip at arm's length, then closer. He sets it on the desk. He looks at Trina, and Trina sets down her tea. "That is a Hardby chop," he says, very evenly. "That is the Hardby chop. The man who put it on this paper is the man who put it on Vandrell's escrow. We have the hand now. We do not have a face — but we have the hand." He does not pick it up again. After a while: "You may have just shortened our work by a season."

C · The locket

  • Investigation DC 14 in the bedchamber: a small silver locket on a fine chain, hanging behind a framed sketch on the wall. Inside: a tiny painted portrait of an unidentified woman, perhaps thirty, dark-haired, with a slight smile. No name. No date.
  • GM: Gilst was unmarried; the woman is not in any Yeomanry register. Leave it a quiet grief — the dead man had a private life nobody else knew. A soft hook for later chapters; nothing to resolve here.

Player handout — the miniature inside the locket.

D · The burned note

  • Investigation DC 13 sifting the cold hearth: a corner of paper survives the burn, partially legible. In an unfamiliar hand, addressed to Gilst, four words remain: "Do not copy eastern marks twice."
  • GM: An instruction Gilst was given by the firm's intermediary. Any copy of an eastern clearing-chop he made was to exist once, on the actual instrument, never as a duplicate in his own files. He wasn't just paid. He was managed.

E · The coin shaving

  • Perception DC 15 along the writing-desk's seam: a single thin curl of silver no larger than a fingernail. Investigation DC 12: a coin shaving — the debris produced when someone clips a coin to private weight, exactly as the countermarked coin on Caelith's table was clipped.
  • GM: Gilst was clipping for the firm too — one more small service, one more small thread they could pull if he ever balked.

F · The initials list

  • Investigation DC 13 in the receipts book's back cover: a separate small list in a different hand. Five sets of initials, each followed by a little thumb-press mark — and one of them, again, simply "E."
  • GM: The other four are intermediaries Gilst never met but whose paydays he knew. The party leaves with a list of letters and no faces. The "E." here is the same "E." from the countersigned receipts — and stays unnamed. (See the @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2DMBriefJrnl.JournalEntryPage.BR2P2DMPgPlotBehind]{What Is Actually Happening} page on the DM Brief journal.)

G · The fear note

  • Perception DC 12 inside the locket-frame: a folded slip of paper tucked behind the painted miniature. Three lines, in Gilst's own hand, undated:

"If they ask again, I do not know how to keep saying no.
If they stop asking, I do not know what that will mean.
This is the third winter I have been afraid."

Player handout — Gilst's note as found, folded behind the locket portrait.

Read in sequence with the rest, the room tells a story: a man paid, then pressured, then asked to do something he tried to refuse, then quietly removed. Coercion, resistance, death.

Caelith, holding the slip: "He was using us before he was using them. He just ran out of time to tell us so."

Before they go

Reith asks two things: that they re-seal the door with their own wax, and that they keep his name out of anything that reaches the watch. He's right to be afraid. (Caelith later sends him a quiet annual stipend through an unnamed channel; Reith never quite works out where it comes from.)

pages.name: Scene 3c · The Physician Two Doors Down — Reln Pollow

text.content:
Scene 3c · The Physician Two Doors Down — Reln Pollow

Reln Pollow's office. He is the next witness who will be killed if not warned in time.

If the party calls on Reln Pollow — the contract physician who certified Gilst's death — they meet a soft, anxious man in his mid-forties with ink-stained fingers and a poorly kept beard, who is far more frightened than a routine three-day-old certification should make anyone.

Pollow's surgery smells of camphor and old paper. He confirms it readily enough — yes, he certified the Gilst man, heart failure, nothing remarkable; the poor fellow was alone in his chair, half a cup of tea gone cold at his elbow. He says it twice, the way people do when they're listening to how it sounds. His hands do not quite stop moving.

He's too nervous (Insight DC 14) — and a closer read (Insight DC 17) tells you the nerves aren't about the death three days ago; they're about something he expects to happen now. (He's right. The conspirators will dispose of him too if the chapter heats up.) Pressed hard (Persuasion DC 18 or Intimidation DC 16) he sweats and gives up nothing actionable, only:

"I did the work I was asked to do. I'm sorry the man is dead. He was kind to me. He gave me a peach last summer."

This is a rescue, not an interrogation

GM: Pollow killed Gilst, but he's the disposable instrument, not the ring above. If the party threatens to pull his license, he begs two days to leave Loftwick. Granting it is the right move — Caelith will hold him to it through Hardby contacts and keep a live witness in play. If the party refuses, Pollow is poisoned the next evening and a witness is lost — but he leaves one trembling line behind, naming his paymaster only as "the man at the Brass Crow." (That's a seed for a much later chapter in Greyhawk; if Pollow dies, make sure the party gets the note.) Either way the scene points the party onward. (Confronting Pollow advances the Pressure Clock one segment — leaning on him is visible from outside.)

Why he was killed (GM-only)

Gilst had become a liability. As long as he was being paid by both sides, he was useful. The moment Cindren & Vhal needed to settle the Vandrell escrow without scrutiny, Gilst became the clerk who could flag the discrepancy in his sleep. "Transferred" in the firm's accounting verb meant he was being moved off active payroll; in fact he was being moved off the world.

The conspirators' instrument of removal: Pollow. The contract physician was paid to certify a heart failure on a man who, by the time he reached his rooms that evening, had drunk a tea spiked with a cardiac glycoside. The death looks natural; the certification is procedurally weak; the Yeomanry's coronial system is being maneuvered around, not corrupted.

What's wrong with the death (forensic backbone)

  • The Yeomanry maintains three coronial physicians on rota. None of the three was called. Pollow is a contract physician; he is licensed, but not on the coronial rota and not authorized to certify deaths-of-civic-record without coronial follow-up. (History DC 12 in the Yeomanry Bench Register confirms.)
  • Pollow's office is two doors down from Gilst's lodgings. He could be on scene within minutes of any call. This is convenient.
  • Pollow's licensure roll, if Caelith pulls it: he qualified in 575 CY in Hardby. He has practiced in Loftwick for nine years. He is not Yeomanry-born.
  • The body was cremated within twelve hours — unusually fast even by Yeomanry custom. The crematorium attendant signed the slip from a chit she does not, on closer inquiry, remember being given.

Caelith, when the party puts the cremation timing together with the contract physician: "He was used. Then he was inconvenient. Then he was not anywhere we could examine."

What the party leaves with

  • Gilst was almost certainly murdered.
  • The murder was procedural, not violent — the Yeomanry's system was used against him, not its blades.
  • Pollow is the disposable instrument; the next ring out is the agent who paid Pollow, and that agent connects upward into Cindren & Vhal Hardby.
  • Phase 2 should not name the agent. The party may chase that thread to Hardby next.

Scene 3d · The Sparrow's Run (the chase)

This is the chapter's one true footrace: an investigation on Wick Lane flushes a lookout the party can chase but cannot catch — and the chase pays them a clue whether or not they ever lay a hand on her. Run it loud and fast and let the table breathe after two movements of quiet paperwork.

How to think about this scene before you run it

GM: The watcher is meant to get away, and she gets away cleanly because she is escaping across her own ground — she used to run messages for a living and she knows the lanes the way the party never will. You can say that out loud; it isn't a cheat, it's her job. The one rule that makes the scene fair: the clue is not the capture. She sheds it partway through the run, so the party recovers it on a win, a loss, or a draw. Catching her is a bonus — and even then she knows almost nothing, so the design never breaks. Put a little Lead tracker on the table (start it at 2); the party drives it down by gaining ground, the watcher drives it up; she escapes when the route runs out. Keep it to three or four rounds and never let one bad roll end it.

Flushing her

The chase begins when the party's work on Wick Lane gives the watcher something worth running to report — most naturally, when they lean on Vesh or come back out of Gilst's sealed rooms carrying something. Give them the catch first:

  • A character keeping half an eye on the street (passive Perception 14, or an active Perception/Insight DC 13) notices the thin young figure from the corner has drifted to keep a clean sightline on the stationer's door, and moves when the party moves.
  • If no one clocks her, she peels off to report the moment the party leaves Gilst's rooms — and someone may catch the movement then (DC 12).
  • And if all else fails, Reith hands her over unprompted: "There's a young one's been hung about the corner since the watch-coat man came. Coyle's girl, I think. Hungry-looking."

The instant the party moves to take her, she runs.

She doesn't startle — she launches, already three strides gone before the nearest of you has shifted weight, ducking under a scrivener's elbow and away down Wick Lane toward the throat of the Cloth Market, with the ease of someone who has run this exact street with the watch behind her. A scrap of oilcloth flaps at her shoulder. She does not look back.

Who she is

Anla "Sparrow" Coyle, about sixteen — wiry, wary, a former message-runner thrown out of work when the little courier-house she ran for folded. A fortnight ago a man she never got a clear look at left coppers and a chalk-stub at a dead-letter niche with a job: watch the stationer's door, mark who goes up to the dead clerk's rooms and who calls on the green-coats, leave a tally every Sevenday, keep nothing. She doesn't know who pays her. She doesn't know what it's for. She knows the lanes of Loftwick better than the watch does, because she used to outrun the watch for a living. She is, deliberately, a frightened kid — which is the conscience this scene is built around.

Sparrow, in brief. AC 12 · HP 9 · Speed 35 ft. · Acrobatics, Stealth, and streetwise all sharp. Nimble Bolt: she Dashes or Disengages as a bonus action every round — the engine of her escape. She carries a fleece-hook and the oilcloth fold, and no weapon she will use on anyone.

The run, beat by beat

Four stretches of Loftwick, each a quick obstacle the party can beat in more than one way. Call for one check per pursuer per stretch; ground gained drops the Lead, ground lost raises it. (Rename the streets to fit your Loftwick if you like — these are placeholders.)

1 · Down Wick Lane into the Cloth Market

She breaks down the length of Wick Lane and spills into the lunchtime crush of the cloth-sellers' square at its foot. The crowd is the obstacle: a pursuer can shove straight through (Athletics), vault a stall (Acrobatics), bellow the crowd aside (Intimidation, or Persuasion — "Make way!"), or read her line and cut the corner (Insight or Investigation). DC 13. She uses the press and her Nimble Bolt to open the gap.

2 · The market wall and the Loft Steps

She makes for a low wall above a worn flight of steps down toward the water — and here is where the scene pays out no matter what. To clear the wall one-handed she slings the oilcloth fold off her shoulder (or it's jolted loose as she goes over); it skids down onto the steps. A pursuer can snatch it up — that costs them their action this round, letting the Lead grow — or mark where it fell and grab it after. Keeping pace over the wall is DC 15 (Athletics or Acrobatics).

GM: The fold drops here, every time. Whether the party catches her, loses her, or breaks off the chase, this is the moment the clue enters their hands or their eyeline. Everything after this is about the person, not the payload. (If the party would rather grab the fold than keep running, that's a perfectly good choice — let them, and let her go.)

3 · The Tanners' cut

Into a reeking alley between the tanneries, where she upends a barrow of fleeces behind her. Pursuers vault or dodge it (Acrobatics or Dexterity), bull straight through (Athletics), or — if anyone knows the district — take the parallel cut and skip the mess (Survival/streetwise). DC 15. This is where she banks her lead; a fumble here costs a pursuer a round untangling from wet wool.

4 · The culvert

The lane ends at the Loft, where a culvert grate stands half-open at the waterline and a flat skiff sits poled and waiting in the reeds. She drops through the grate — too narrow for an armored adult — or into the skiff and out under a drain-arch into the dark of the under-streets.

GM: This is her escape, and it should usually work. Unless the party has already driven the Lead to zero and has a real way to follow — a flying or Small companion, a readied grapple, a hold person or misty step, water-walking — she is gone. If a player spends a genuine resource to cut the run short earlier (a spell, a clever readied action, Trina's help if she's nearby and willing), reward it generously: drop the Lead by two, or let them skip a stretch. The feeling you want is the party did everything right and still met a professional on her home street.

What she dropped — the fold

Player handout — the fold as the party finds it on the Loft Steps.

A flat oilcloth packet: a stub of chalk, a notched tally-stick, a half-written report-slip, and a folded instruction-card. Read it in layers, the way the party read the table:

  • What it is (Investigation DC 10): surveillance. Someone has been logging who visits the stationer's door and the green-coats' office. The half-written slip even carries the party's own descriptions — and a line above them: "watch-coat man — 1 hr — took nothing." (That's the thief's recon. The party realizes the firm has had this street under glass for days.)
  • The drop (Investigation DC 13, the instruction-card): she leaves her tallies at a dead-letter niche in the city — behind the loose ashlar at the Coopers' Arms, on the Tannerway corner — to be lifted each Sevenday. "Keep nothing." That's an immediate Loftwick thread: stake out the niche, trace the hand that empties it.
  • The route (History or Investigation DC 16, the routing mark): the card bears a small clearing-glyph kin to the firm's tower-over-waves mark, and the line "east on the Sevenday cart." The reports don't stop in Loftwick — they ride to Hardby. Sparrow is the near end of a courier chain whose far end is the firm's Hardby hand.

Two pointers, guaranteed: a niche to watch here, and a road that runs east. The fold names a route and a firm-mark — never "E.," never the tube's strange craft, never a throne. It points at the road, not the mastermind.

How it ends

However the run plays out, it lands somewhere clean:

  • She's gone, and you have the fold (the usual outcome). A fair loss with a real prize — and the firm doesn't yet know its watcher is blown. The party leaves Wick Lane with the east-pointer in hand.
  • You catch her (only with real effort). You have the fold and a terrified sixteen-year-old who can tell you only that "a man left coppers at the drop — I never saw his face." Now the scene's quiet test: what do they do with her? Trina will take her in without being asked — "I have a back room, and a use for someone who knows the lanes." Caelith will quietly relocate and pay her, the way he handles Reith. Roughing her up costs the party nothing on the sheet and something in the room, and a watching Trina remembers it. This is the conscience the chase exists to carry. (Her capture blows the firm's surveillance — bring the squeeze of Movement Three a little forward.)
  • You lose both (you broke off, or the dice were cruel). The fold dropped at the Loft Steps regardless; that afternoon a fleece-seller returns it to the party — "a runner dropped this; looked official" — or Caelith's quiet contact does. The road still runs east.
  • You never engaged her at all. She tails the party home, and her report is what tips the firm into the night's events. She turns up on Wick Lane again later for a second look. Nothing dead-ends.

A harder target, if your table wants teeth

GM: Swap Sparrow for the adult "leg" the firm actually runs its chain through — the runner Vesh sends out the back. Same four stretches, but the escape is a pre-positioned skiff rather than a child's culvert, and cornering him yields a description that matches the Hardby broker's circle (a stronger seed for the next chapter) but no name, under any roll — he's paid for silence the way professionals are. The trade is menace for conscience; run one or the other, not both.

On the table (Foundry)

GM: Use the Sparrow's Run — Loftwick scene with token vision on; lay a light chain of street walls along Wick Lane → the Cloth Market → the Loft Steps → the Tanners' cut → the culvert, and mark the four stretches and the fold-drop. (The map's own notes already flag it for exactly this.)

Sparrow — scene assets

  • Sparrow actor: @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-actors.Actor.BR2P2ASprrwCoyle]{Anla "Sparrow" Coyle} — drop her token onto the chase scene to run the four stretches.
  • Chase scene: @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-scenes.Scene.BR2SceneSprwLftwk]{Sparrow's Run — Loftwick} — the laid-out four-stretch chase map with the fold-drop marked.
  • Player handout: the fold image above (modules/blooming-rot-2/assets/handouts/sparrow-fold.png) can be shown to the table when the fold lands on the Loft Steps.

Where this leads

Whatever else happened, the party walks off Wick Lane with the road drawn for them — the Hardby chop, the words "Hardby work," and "east on the Sevenday cart," all pointing the same way. Now the other neighborhood, where the conspiracy has a face and a family.

Scene 4 · Tannerway

A workaday street by the eastern caravan yard — leather, grain, and the smell of the road. Where the missing woman lived and worked.

If Wick Lane is paper and pursuit, Tannerway is people. Nothing here is a trap and nothing here runs; this is where the party stops chasing a ledger and starts chasing a woman with a family and a clock on her. Three doors, and the stakes that turn the party's curiosity into urgency.

The three Tannerway scenes — the counting house, the schoolmaster's house, the watch house — each have their own page on this journal. Run them in any order. Land the final beat (The verb that matters) somewhere across the visit, in whichever room makes Caelith's distinction hurt most.

The verb that matters

Somewhere in working Tannerway, the party should land on the line that makes the whole travel choice hurt. Tamsin is MORAVEN delayed — not settled. Hold Caelith to the distinction:

"I do not say it as a comfort. I say it because the verb is not 'settled.' She is somewhere they have not yet decided to close her account. They will, eventually, decide. Whether they decide before you reach her depends on how you travel."

Alive, and on a clock. That is the stake the party carries to the final scene.

Where Movement Two leads

When the party pools what both neighborhoods gave them, don't have Caelith announce the destination — lay the threads side by side and let them draw the line. Gilst's bank instrument is a Hardby chop. Vesh said Hardby work. The chase fold says east on the Sevenday cart. Tamsin's last queries were Hardby queries. Ailen is in Hardby. And Veska Maelan — the trade-clearing notary Caelith named on the night they took the commission — is in Hardby, and can read these chops better than anyone outside the firm. Six threads, one city. The party will see it. That recognition is the motion this chapter was built to produce.

pages.name: Scene 4a · The Counting House — Doril Veth

text.content:
Scene 4a · The Counting House — Doril Veth

Doril Veth at the standing desk; Tamsin's chair empty behind him.

Tamsin Moraven's counting house stands open and quietly grieving, run in her absence by her chief clerk, Doril Veth, who keeps her chair empty behind the standing desk and has been cataloguing her unfinished work because he expects her to want to resume it.

A narrow counting room: a standing desk at the front, a weighbench at the back, bound carbons in a wall niche, and behind the desk a good chair no one is sitting in. The man at the ledgers is perhaps fifty, careful, red-eyed in the way of someone who has decided not to cry at work. "You're here about Mistress Moraven," he says, before you've said so. "Good. Somebody should be." He turns the open book toward you. "She was in the middle of something. People in the middle of something do not simply walk off to a farm."

What the last ledger shows

  • Veth will show the party her last open ledger and help them read it. Investigation DC 12: her final three queries all reference Cindren & Vhal Hardby chops.
  • Investigation DC 15: her own carbon copies of two of those queries are gone — lifted from the bound book and replaced with blank impression-pages. Someone reached into this room before the party did.

The claims-before-loss pattern

If a character follows her trail of questions to its end (Investigation DC 16, or DC 14 with Trina reading alongside), the pattern she was building toward surfaces, and it is the worst thing in the chapter:

The claims were filed before the loss occurred.

The insurance claims on three South Province grain shipments — for storms, spoilage, seizure — were filed before the losses happened. By eleven days in one case; by three weeks in another.

Caelith, when the party brings him the pattern: "That is not theft. That is being paid to know what tomorrow looks like. Whoever ran this had a way to make tomorrow obey them."

Read forward it's fraud; read backward it's something colder — the losses were arranged, the claims pre-positioned. Prediction, profit, erasure. Tamsin saw it. Then Tamsin vanished.

Who Tamsin was

Tamsin Moraven, mid-fifties, a senior grain factor in the Yeomanry. Twelve years in independent trade; before that, she clerked for the Loftwick Treasury's Grain Office. She grades and contracts harvest sales for export — most of her business is moving Yeomanry winter wheat and durum to Aerdi-coastal buyers. Her counting house is on Tannerway, near the eastern caravan yard.

Husband: Vellin Moraven, a quiet schoolmaster at a small private school. Eldest daughter: Ailen Moraven, mid-twenties, now studying with a sculptor in Hardby (her tuition co-sponsored years ago by Trina Alvere and Tamsin herself).

pages.name: Scene 4b · The Schoolmaster's House — Vellin Moraven

text.content:
Scene 4b · The Schoolmaster's House — Vellin Moraven

Vellin Moraven holding the note Tamsin left. Heartbreak quietly carried.

A short walk from the counting house, Tamsin's husband keeps their small house. Vellin Moraven is a quiet schoolmaster who has aged a year in eleven days, and he carries his fear so gently that it's worse to watch than weeping.

The schoolmaster receives you in a front parlor that is too tidy, the tidiness of a man who has nothing to do with his hands. He offers tea he forgets to pour. When you ask about his wife he is quiet a moment, then takes a slip of paper from the desk — already worn at the folds, as if it's been opened many times — and lays it flat for you to read. One line, in a firm woman's hand: "If I am not back by Sevenday, take Ailen out of Hardby." "Ailen is our daughter," he says. "She's studying with a sculptor. In Hardby." He does not finish the thought. He hasn't been able to write the letter.

What the note does

The Sevenday note does two things at once:

  • It confirms Tamsin knew she was in danger and went anyway. She walked into whatever swallowed her with her eyes open.
  • It puts a name and a face in Hardby — their daughter Ailen, unprotected, in the city the whole chapter points toward.

Gently suggesting Vellin send for her tonight is the right move (Insight DC 12 tells the party as much). He has not yet sent for Ailen because he could not bring himself to write the letter.

How Trina helps

If Trina is with the party she'll write to Ailen herself, by her own courier — a poet she sponsors, leaving for Hardby that very night — and the letter may pay off when the party reaches Hardby in the next chapter. Trina knew Tamsin slightly and Ailen well; Vellin will recognize her and accept tea from her hand when he would not from a stranger.

Tactical Map

Vellin Moraven's home — tactical map. Vellin and his daughter Ailen live here; small, well-kept.

pages.name: Scene 4c · The Watch House — Sergeant Halir Anwic

text.content:
Scene 4c · The Watch House — Sergeant Halir Anwic

If the party calls at the watch house, they find Sergeant Halir Anwic, honest, overworked, and quietly ashamed of how thin his report on Moraven is.

The sergeant doesn't make excuses, which is somehow worse. "No body, no struggle, no weight behind the name," he says, reciting the categories that let a file go cold. "So I marked it open and unescalated and I moved on to the ones that were shouting at me." He rubs his face. "I didn't look hard. I should have. Tell me you're looking, and I'll look with you."

What Anwic will and won't do

  • Anwic will reopen the file at Caelith's word — not before. He's a careful man, and a missing-person file in his book isn't his to reopen on a stranger's say-so. (One short letter from Caelith does it.)
  • Once it's reopened, he's a Watch ally worth having: he's been pacing the Tannerway end of the city for eleven days carrying this on his shoulders, and he'll work hard to make it right.
  • If the chapter's pressure later forces him off the case — a reassignment, a quiet word from above — he finds the party privately first and presses his notes into their hands as a parting gift.

What the file actually says

Anwic's report is what the records alcove already showed: eleven days ago Moraven left her counting house with a travel bag and a heavy cloak, told her staff she was riding to a customer's farm, did not name the farm, and did not return. Open, unescalated. "Subject left counting house with bag and cloak; staff state she was due at a customer farm; staff cannot name the farm. No body. No struggle. Open file."

If asked, he says it plainly: "I did not look hard. I should have. I am looking now."

Interlude · The Mentor (cross-ref)

Woven through this whole Movement, not a scene of its own, is the slow, quiet thing between Trina Alvere and the party's warlock, Alicia. It is the soft counterweight to all this administrative cold; ration it — one beat per session, no more — so each lands with weight.

The four-beat sequence (the first recognition; the gift of the moth scarf; the corridor word after Moll; the vellum book of clerks' poems) lives in full on Trina's own journal. Use this page only to remember that the beats belong here, not in the Movement One brief or the Movement Three squeeze.

GM: See @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2TrinaJournl.JournalEntryPage.BR2P2TrPgAlicia1]{Trina & Alicia — the four beats} for the full text, the gift's mechanics, and the things never to name in any of these rooms.
